---
title: "Margins"
subtitle: "The early-modern Hebrew print corpus, seen through the Bibliography of the Hebrew Book"
---

This site accompanies the **Margins** ERC application. It documents the corpus the project will work with, the data the project will build on, and the data-model gaps the project proposes to close.

The Margins corpus is defined in the proposal as **all books and broadsides printed in Hebrew fonts, from the beginning of Hebrew printing until the end of the 18th century** — c.1470–1800. This site explores that window through the **Bibliography of the Hebrew Book (BHB)**, the National Library of Israel's catalogue of Hebrew-letter print before 1960, and the **DiJeSt** project that mapped and Kima-aligned its place data.

## What you'll find here

### [The Corpus](corpus/index.qmd)

A dashboard of the BHB restricted to the Margins window. Adds three layers to the existing DiJeSt visualization:

- **Time as a structural axis** — the print-productivity curve 1470–1800 with the 1630s Thirty Years' War dip and the late-18th-c. acceleration.
- **Paratext as data** — the haskama (rabbinic approbation) and chronogram (title-page letter-dating) layers already structured in BHB fields 915 and 912, charted over space and time.
- **Bowers/Gaskell signals** — the issue / state / cancel / variant / impression distinctions that live in free-text notes, surfaced as a study sample with the parsing grammar made explicit.

### [Desiderata](desiderata/index.qmd)

What Margins will add to the existing data. A coverage matrix of MARC fields against the DiJeSt crosswalk — green where the target ontology (FOAF / BIBO / FABIO / Kima / Wikidata) already maps, red where the white space lies. One worked example transforms a real BHB free-text note (Salonika 1594) into structured JSON and then into LRMoo Turtle, demonstrating the project's data-engineering contribution at the level of a single record.

### [Methods](methods/bibliographic_units_and_marc.qmd)

The conceptual scaffolding behind the dashboard: how Work / Expression / Manifestation / Item map (and fail to map) to MARC; how Bowers/Gaskell distinctions live in free-text notes; what ontologies already model these distinctions; and the Hebrew terminology that the BHB cataloguers actually use.

### [About](about.qmd)

Acknowledgements (DiJeSt, KIMA, Sharon Kurant, the BHB project) and the data, code, and licensing notes.

## Sister sites

The original DiJeSt Looker Studio dashboard remains the canonical **spatial** view across the full BHB span (1450–1991). It is embedded into the Corpus page where relevant and linked throughout.

→ [DiJeSt: Bibliography of the Hebrew Book, visualized](https://datastudio.google.com/u/0/reporting/73a17dcc-f092-4dec-8c57-58b606862b2d/page/FPCXD)
